title: Chocolate courgette cake
subtitle: Dense, moist chocolate loaf with grated courgette folded through the batter.
excerpt: Chocolate courgette cake uses grated courgette to keep the crumb moist for several days. The courgette is grated finely and squeezed dry before folding in; it disappears into the batter and you cannot taste it. The result is a deeply chocolatey loaf that slices cleanly and keeps well.
sourceNotes: Courgette in baked goods follows the same tradition as carrot cake: a vegetable grated and folded into a fat-based batter provides moisture and a fine crumb. Courgette bread was popular in American home baking from the 1970s, when home vegetable gardens regularly produced a surplus of courgettes. The chocolate version became a standard way to use a glut of large courgettes. This recipe follows the American quick-bread method adapted to a British loaf tin and ingredient proportions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice, wrap individual pieces in cling film, and freeze for up to two months. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: This cake keeps well for three to four days wrapped at room temperature. The crumb actually improves on the second day.

content.text: Grated courgette acts like moisture insurance in this cake. It releases water slowly during baking, keeping the crumb soft and dense rather than dry. Squeeze the grated courgette in a clean tea towel before adding it; if you skip this step the batter becomes too wet and the cake sinks.

items.prepNote: medium, coarsely grated and squeezed dry

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line a 900g loaf tin.
Grate {{courgette}} coarsely, then tip into a clean tea towel and squeeze hard to remove as much liquid as possible. Set aside.
Put {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl and whisk to mix. Add {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and whisk it in well.
In a separate bowl, whisk {{sunflower-oil}}, {{eggs}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} together until combined. Pour into the dry ingredients and stir until just combined.
Fold in the squeezed courgette and the {{dark-chocolate}} chunks if using. The batter will be thick.

Spoon into the prepared tin and level the top. Bake for 60 to 65 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with just a few moist crumbs. Cover loosely with foil after 40 minutes if the top is browning quickly.
Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then transfer to a rack.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: This is a forgiving bake with a couple of common pitfalls.

items.cause: Courgette not squeezed dry, making the batter too wet.
items.fix: Squeeze harder next time. The courgette should feel almost dry to the touch before it goes in. A slightly sunken loaf still tastes fine; the texture is unaffected.

items.symptom: Dense and gummy rather than moist.
items.fix: The skewer test is essential with this loaf. Stir the wet and dry ingredients until just combined; a few streaks of flour are acceptable.
